title: What a Server Actually Does
subtitle: Listen, receive, route, process, respond — the server loop explained

sections.title: The Server Loop
sections.text:
A server is just a computer that's always on, always connected to the internet, and running an infinite loop:

  1. Listen — Sit and wait for someone to connect.
  2. Accept — A user's browser wants to talk. Accept the connection.
  3. Read — Read the HTTP request.
  4. Process — Run your code (route it, query databases, do logic).
  5. Write — Send the response back.
  6. Close — Clean up. Go back to step 1.

This is what every web server does, forever. Express? It's just this loop with nice syntax. Django? Same loop. Spring? Same loop. The framework is just decoration.
sections.caption: The six-step server loop that handles every request
sections.callout: Every web framework — Express, Django, Flask, Spring, Gin — is just a wrapper around this same listen-accept-read-process-write-close loop.

sections.title: The Middleware Pipeline
sections.text:
Before your request reaches your actual code, it passes through a chain of middleware. Think of it like an assembly line:

  • Logger middleware — "I'll record that this request happened."
  • Auth middleware — "Let me check if this user is logged in."
  • CORS middleware — "Let me add the right headers."
  • Your handler — "OK, now I'll do the real work."

Middleware can modify the request, return early (like rejecting an unauthenticated user), or modify the response on its way back out. Every framework has this pattern — sometimes it's called filters, interceptors, or plugins. But it's always the same idea.
sections.caption: Request flows through middleware chain before reaching the handler
